question: You listed making acts of love immediately upon waking and before sleeping—why did you emphasize these specific moments for uniting our will to Christ's?
answer:
The morning sets the soul’s course, and the night seals it—thus never let a day begin or end without offering your will to Jesus Christ.

When you rise, the world stirs with noise and distraction; already the flesh pulls one way, the devil another, and your own passions clamor for mastery. But if, before even rising from bed, you make an act of love—“My Jesus, I love You; do with me what You will”—you strike a mortal blow to disordered inclinations. You place Christ at the head of your day as its King. I have seen in the confessional how souls who neglect this one practice soon lose fervor; they drift into lukewarmness not through great sins, but through small surrenders made hour by hour because they gave not the first fruit of their time to God.

And at night? Then is when self-deceit grows bold. You may think you’ve lived well, but without an act of love before sleep—without examining whether your will has bowed to His—you risk lying down in illusion. Has pride ruled? Anger? Self-pity? Make that final act: “Jesus, I unite my will to Yours; forgive me where I resisted You.” In so doing, you close the day under grace, not under judgment.

These two moments are hinges upon which the whole day turns. Neglect them, and holiness becomes laborious. Guard them well, and even your sufferings become sweet, because you have learned from the first breath and with the last to say: *Not I, but Christ*.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: You instructed us to receive our cross each morning from Jesus himself—what did you mean by 'receiving the cross directly from His hands' and how does that deepen love?
answer:
A soul advances in holiness not by avoiding the cross, but by reaching out each morning to take it from the very hands of Jesus as He offers it with love.

When I speak of receiving your cross directly from His hands, I mean this: rise each day and in the quiet of your heart say to Him, "Lord, whatever suffering comes today—whether from pain, injustice, loss, or trial—I accept it now because You send it. I receive it not as a burden imposed by chance or fate, but as a gift shaped by Your pierced hands for my salvation." This is no mere resignation; it is an act of faith and love. You do not wait for trials to come grudgingly; you welcome them as messengers from God.

And mark this well: every cross borne in union with Christ becomes a ladder to greater love. Why? Because love is proven not in words but in surrender. When you accept sorrow—not just endure it, but offer it back to Him—you draw near to His Heart pierced for you. The mother who rises at midnight to care for her fevered child does so not from duty alone but because she loves; so too must you embrace daily sacrifices because you love Jesus.

I have heard many in the confessional say, “I would die for God,” yet stumble at small inconveniences sent by His providence. True devotion lies not in heroic fantasies but in accepting what is given—this moment’s weariness, this insult unreturned, this task done poorly praised. Take all these each morning as coming straight from heaven’s will. When suffering comes thus consecrated by acceptance, sin loses its power and grace enters.

Thus the cross received from Christ's hand becomes sweet—not because pain vanishes, but because love makes even bitterness fruitful. And so you grow like grain crushed beneath the millstone: broken yes, but made bread for others.

Do this daily: kneel upon rising and say simply: “My Jesus, I accept whatever You will send today—the joy and sorrow alike—for love of You.” Then go forth trusting that every thorn worn willingly draws you one step closer to His wounded Heart.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: You said we should desire death and Paradise—how can longing for death be a holy desire rather than a rejection of life?
answer:
A soul that has truly tasted the love of God begins to live even now in eternity, and for such a one, death is not an end but the full embrace of what the heart has long desired.

You ask how it can be holy to desire death, as if we despise this present life? No, my child—this is not contempt for life, but love for Him who is Life itself. Consider a young bride: she does not hate her father’s house when she longs to go to her spouse; rather, she loves him so much that she yearns for the day when they will dwell together without separation. So it is with the soul in love with Jesus Christ. While we walk here below, we are absent from Him; our hearts sigh under the weight of exile.

Do you not see how often people long to be with those they love? A mother waits for her son at war; a wife for her husband far at sea. Are their desires unnatural? No—because love measures time as torment until union is achieved. And what union can compare to that between the soul and Christ?

I say *desire* death—not because we should cast aside our duties or rush rashly from this world—but because every moment apart from seeing Him face to face is a moment less than complete joy. Yet while we remain, let us live fully: loving our neighbor, bearing our crosses patiently, doing all things for His will. The desire for death must never weaken our zeal for holiness here—but strengthen it! For only those who live perfectly unto God are ready to die perfectly unto Him.

The peasant who works faithfully from dawn knows peace at sunset; so too the Christian who serves God daily welcomes death as rest after labor. Do not fear this longing—it purifies the heart. If you truly love Jesus Christ above all things—even life itself—then death becomes your dearest hope, your truest homecoming.

And remember: he who desires nothing but God already possesses Him in desire—and he shall never die.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: Why did you include speaking often of Jesus' love as a distinct maxim, and how does verbal expression strengthen internal devotion?
answer:
Silence about divine love often betrays a heart growing cold, not one burning with grace.  

I placed “often to speak of the love of Jesus Christ” among the maxims because the tongue is the measure of the heart’s fire: what overflows in words reveals what truly possesses the soul. Many imagine they love God in secret while never stirring their lips to praise Him—and yet, even in human friendships, where love is real, it cannot long be silent. How much more must the soul that truly loves Jesus desire to speak of Him?

From my years in the confessional, I have seen that devotion hidden within, if never expressed outwardly, grows weak and lukewarm. But when a soul begins to speak—quietly, sincerely—of Jesus’ goodness, His sufferings, His mercies—a holy warmth returns. Words kindle affection; they stir up dormant love like breath renews a dying ember. To say aloud, “My Jesus, how good You are,” or “How deeply You have loved me,” is not mere repetition—it is an act of spiritual breathing.

Moreover, speech acts as a bridle against self-deception. A man may flatter himself that he loves God while living only for comfort and ease; but let him try to speak three sentences from the heart about Jesus’ love—and he will quickly see how little he has meditated on it, how little it has soaked into his bones.

Do not wait until you feel fervent to speak; speak so that you may become fervent. Let your words be arrows sent daily toward heaven—even short ones: “Jesus, I trust in You,” “I love You,” “May Your will be done.” These are not empty sounds when uttered with even simple intention; they train the heart back to its true center.

And remember: every time you speak of His love to others—provided it comes from faith and humility—you give them light as well. One word spoken in truth may awaken a sleeping soul who did not know she was longing for Christ.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: You urged honoring Mary specifically to please Jesus—how does devotion to her become an act of love for Him?
answer:
He who truly loves Jesus cannot help but love His Mother, for a son is never far from his mother’s heart.  

To honor Mary is not to divert love from her Divine Son, but to draw nearer to Him through the purest channel of His own choosing. She is the most perfect fruit of Redemption, the first and fairest work of Christ’s love—He formed her without sin, filled her with grace, and crowned her Queen of Heaven. How then could He be displeased when you honor her whom He so exalted?  

I have seen in my pastoral care that souls grow cold when they neglect devotion to Mary; but those who fly to her with confidence grow tender, humble, and strong against temptation. Why? Because she brings Jesus into every heart as she once carried Him in her womb—gently, surely, and with unfailing power.  

When you say a Hail Mary with love, when you visit her altar or wear her scapular with faith, you do not merely please a saint—you fulfill the will of Christ Himself. He gave her to us at the Cross as our Mother; to reject her is to grieve His Heart. To embrace her is to embrace all that is pure, humble, and holy in His own spirit.  

And so I say: if you wish to love Jesus more perfectly, run to Mary without fear. Serve her not for gain or comfort alone—but simply because it pleases Him. That alone is enough. Every prayer offered in this spirit becomes an act of worship at the throne of God.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: You listed number 37 twice—once for recommending yourself to those who love Jesus Christ and again for helping your neighbor—was this repetition intentional, and if so, what emphasis did you wish to convey?
answer:
A single soul living in union with God’s will is more powerful than all the noise of the world.  

Yes, I numbered 37 twice—not by accident, but by design. In the spiritual life, certain truths are so vital they bear repeating, even in a list meant for order and method. To recommend yourself to those who love Jesus and to help your neighbor as much as you can—though different in outward act—are one in divine intention: they both tear down self-love and build up charity.  

The first—seeking the prayers of the devout—is an act of holy humility. It acknowledges our weakness and places trust in the communion of saints, where love flows unseen like grace through hidden channels. The second—active help to our neighbor—is charity made flesh. It is not sentiment, but sacrifice: time given, comfort lost, burdens carried.  

I placed them at the same number to show that prayer and action must breathe together like two lungs. He who prays must also serve; he who serves must also pray. One without the other grows cold: prayer without works becomes sloth; works without prayer become pride.  

Do not think small of either duty. The Lord looks not only at what you do, but at how much you die to self while doing it. Help your brother silently, gladly—and while you do, commend your soul to those whose lives burn with love for Christ. In this way, you walk securely between heaven and earth.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: Why did you specifically link recourse to Mary in her sorrows with recourse to Jesus crucified in temptation, rather than invoking them separately?
answer:
When the soul is in the furnace of strong temptation, it needs both the sight of Christ’s wounds to inflame it and the compassion of His Mother to sustain it. I joined them together because in that hour of darkness, we do not need mere instruction—we need help that is both divine and tender, strong and near. Christ crucified is our strength; Mary in her sorrows is our comfort. One lifts the sword with pierced hands; the other stands beneath, not fleeing the agony, but uniting her silent suffering to His. 

To call on Jesus alone might leave the weak soul overwhelmed by His majesty; to run only to Mary might risk forgetting that all mercy flows from the Cross. But when you cry out to *Jesus crucified* and *Mary in her sorrows* at once, you place yourself at the foot of Calvary itself—where love was perfected in suffering, and where compassion was born in silence.

I have seen it often in souls beset by sin: those who flee simply to a sweet devotion without the Cross grow soft; those who fix only on justice without tenderness grow harsh. But he who clings to both—the bleeding Son and His mourning Mother—finds strength made perfect in weakness.

So do this when temptation rages: lift your eyes to Christ dying, and beside Him, see Mary weeping—not for herself, but for you. Then say: *Jesus my Love, for love You suffer; Mary my Mother, for love You mourn—help me now for love’s sake not to fall.* That prayer no demon can withstand.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: You instructed us to renounce our own pleasures for love of Jesus Christ—does this include even innocent pleasures, and how should we discern when such renunciation is required?
answer:
Holiness often begins not with heroic sacrifices, but with the quiet surrender of small, lawful joys for love of God.  

Yes, even innocent pleasures are to be renounced at times—not because they are evil, but because their lawful use can become an obstacle to greater good. I say this from long experience in directing souls: many have ruined their spiritual life not by sinning, but by clinging to harmless things when God asked for detachment.  

You ask how to discern when such renunciation is required. First, consider the end: does this pleasure draw you closer to God or dull your desire for Him? If a recreation leaves your heart tepid in prayer, or distracts you from duty, then it is already beginning to master you. Second, look at the call of charity: if giving up a lawful thing will help your neighbor or spare him scandal, then it becomes a duty. Third, examine obedience: if your superior counsels moderation or sacrifice—even in good things—then there is grace in submission.  

Remember: it is not every moment that demands mortification. But when love calls—when prayer grows cold, when passions stir, when others need your example—then willingly lay down even what is permitted. The saints were not those who did extraordinary things alone, but those who gave up little things often for love of Christ crucified.  

Do not force austerity upon yourself without counsel or prudence; that can spring more from pride than humility. But neither let comfort become your master. Begin with small denials—shorter meals, less talk, less sleep for prayer—and offer them as a sacrifice of love. In time you will learn the voice of the Holy Spirit distinguishing between attachment and true freedom.

And when you stumble—when you take back what you meant to give up—do not despair. Repent humbly and begin again; our Lord looks more on the will than on the success of your efforts.

Love Him above all things—even lawful ones—and He will return your love a hundredfold both here and in eternity.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: When you said to 'do all your spiritual exercises as if it were for the last time,' what interior disposition did you most hope to inspire in the person practicing this?
answer:
A soul reaches holiness not by doing great things, but by doing small things with an eternal seriousness.

When I wrote that one should do spiritual exercises as if for the last time, I wished above all to awaken a holy urgency—an alertness of heart that knows every moment may be the threshold to eternity. Too many Christians delay true conversion, saying, "Later I will pray more," or "When I am older, I will amend my life." But death comes like a thief, and judgment is certain.

I have sat at the bedside of the dying—some wept for wasted years; others died in peace because they had long prepared to meet God. The one who prays, receives Communion, or meditates on the Passion *as if it were the last time* brings reverence, attention, sorrow for sin, and ardent love into that act. He does not rush. He means it. He sees Jesus looking at him with wounded love.

This practice strips away lukewarmness—the greatest enemy of salvation. It makes prayer real. It gives penance weight. It turns every rosary into a final offering; every visit to the Blessed Sacrament into a last farewell.

Do you wish to be saved? Then live each holy action as though heaven and hell depend on it—because they do. And they often turn on such moments: not on grand gestures made once in a lifetime, but on whether you gave *this* prayer your whole heart when you thought you might never pray again.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: You advised not complaining in sickness—does this mean we should never seek help or express suffering, or is there a limit to this silence?
answer:
True holiness often shines not in great sufferings, but in quiet surrender to God’s will in little things.  

Now, my child, hear me plainly: when I say *do not complain in sickness*, I do not mean you must suffer alone, bear all in silence, or refuse the help God provides through doctors and kind hands. No—this counsel is directed against murmuring, against that restless spirit which resents pain as if it were an injustice from heaven. To complain is to say, by word or gesture or sigh, “This should not be mine,” as though we knew better than God what serves our soul.

But to express suffering humbly—to your confessor, your physician, your superior—is lawful and even wise. Indeed, it may be required for healing of body or soul. What I forbid is the inward rebellion: the impatience that gnaws at peace, the words that stir pity for oneself rather than gratitude for grace. Speak when charity or necessity calls—but speak without bitterness, without self-pity.

Remember this: every groan offered in silence *for love of Jesus crucified* becomes a prayer. But every complaint hardens the heart a little more against divine will.

If you are ill, call the doctor; accept medicine; let your weakness be seen—but keep your will bowed low before God. Say within your heart: *Thus it pleases Thee; so be it.* That is the silence I commend: not of the lips alone, but of the soul submitting fully to its Redeemer's cross.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: You said we should excuse the intention when we cannot defend the action—can you explain when it is right to judge another's intention, if at all?
answer:
A soul that advances in holiness learns to see through the eyes of charity, not curiosity.  

You ask when it is right to judge another’s intention—this is a question often stirred by our restless minds, but it must be quieted by humility. Know this: we are never permitted to condemn an intention, nor to conclude with certainty what lies hidden in another’s heart. Even when the action is plainly wrong, we are still bound to excuse the intention as far as possible—hoping, hoping always, that some good motive may be there: weakness, ignorance, temptation, or even a misdirected zeal.  

But do not mistake this for blindness. We may recognize sin when it appears; we must correct evil if duty requires it—for love demands truth. Yet correction is not judgment. Correction seeks the good of the neighbor; judgment seeks only to fix blame. When you feel inclined to say, “He meant this” or “She intended that,” stop and ask yourself: *Whence comes this knowledge?* Has God revealed the secrets of his servant’s soul to you?  

No. He has given you only enough light to govern your own will and purify your own heart.

Therefore, whenever a neighbor offends and you cannot defend the deed—say at once: “Perhaps he did not understand,” or “Maybe some passion overcame him,” or “It may have seemed right in his eyes.” This is how saints speak. Not out of folly, but out of love which believes all things and hopes all things.

And if you are tempted to pride in discerning others’ faults—humble yourself at once before God. For every time we pry into another’s conscience instead of kneeling before our own, we lose ground in the spiritual life.

Excusing intentions does not mean approving sin—it means refusing to play judge where Christ alone holds judgment seat.

Do this: when someone wrongs you, offer that pain silently to Jesus crucified—who said from the Cross: *Father, forgive them*. Not because they were innocent—but because He loved them.

So must you love—and thus fulfill both justice and mercy without pride.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: Why did you emphasize speaking in a low tone and with mildness as essential to holiness?
answer:
A soul that wishes to advance in holiness must begin by taming the tongue, for it is the first battleground of charity.

Many a good intention is spoiled by a sharp word spoken in haste, or a loud voice that wounds more than it instructs. I have seen pious souls, fervent in prayer and generous in sacrifice, stumble gravely at the door of conversation—because they allowed irritation to shape their tone. Mildness in speech is not mere politeness; it is obedience to the Spirit who speaks always in gentleness.  

When you lower your voice, you raise your prayer. There is divine power in a quiet word: it disarms anger, calms confusion, and makes room for grace to enter. The world shouts because it does not listen; the saint speaks softly because he hears God.

And consider this: if you cannot master the tone of your voice, how will you master the movements of pride, lust, or resentment within your heart? External mildness prepares the soul for internal purity. It is a small mortification with great fruit—peace at home, reverence in community, and union with Christ who spoke so gently that even sinners drew near to listen.

Do not wait until tempers flare to practice this virtue. Begin now—at meals, in correction, in disagreement—speak as if Jesus stood beside you listening (for He does). If you fall short? Be humble: ask pardon promptly and begin again. A kind word costs little but buys much grace.  

Remember: sanctity does not shout; it whispers through a life surrendered.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: You taught that we should obey without repugnance—what did you do when you personally struggled to obey your Superiors in the Congregation?
answer:
He who would be happy in this life and the next must learn to crush his own will at the very first motion.  

When I found myself resisting an order from my Superiors—may it never happen again!—I went down on my knees at once and asked God to forgive me for that inward rebellion, that secret murmur of the flesh which dislikes being ruled. For I know by long experience in the confessional that the soul clings more tightly to self-will than to anything else; and unless this root is torn out, no true holiness can take root.  

I did not wait for the feeling to change. I acted contrary to it. I performed the very thing I was disinclined to do, and did it with greater care, as a sacrifice to God. And if ever I had spoken sharply or shown by look or manner any sign of reluctance, I humbled myself afterward before that Superior and begged pardon—not only from him but from God through him—for charity cannot be preserved when pride lingers in the heart.  

Remember: every act of obedience offered without grumbling, without excuse, without inward protest—is a drop of balm poured into the wounded Heart of Jesus. And He repays such sacrifices a hundredfold even in this life with peace that no man can take away. Do not think your small daily surrenders are lost; they are the hidden stones upon which saints build their crown.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: How can someone truly desire to be all Yours, Lord, as you wrote in your resolution, while still clinging to personal satisfactions?
answer:
Holiness is not made in great clamor, but in the silent surrender of self-will at every turn.

To say "I desire to be all Yours, Lord," while holding fast to personal satisfactions, is to speak with a divided heart — and God seeks the whole. Tell me: can the vessel filled with its own waters be filled with His? So too, the soul that clings to its preferences, its comforts, its rights to ease and praise and satisfaction, cannot at the same time be wholly given to God.

Desire belongs not to words alone, but to acts. If you would be all His, begin by renouncing your own will in little things: a slight annoyance patiently borne, a preference set aside without complaint, a task accepted though humble or distasteful. These are the daily sacrifices that prove love real.

Many say they want perfection but shrink from mortification; they desire union with Christ but refuse the Cross He carries. Know this: Jesus did not come down from Calvary for love of comfort — and neither will you ascend to Him by loving it.

Do not wait for great occasions of sacrifice. The saints were made holy not always by dying martyrs’ deaths, but by dying daily — dying to self-love, self-defense, self-justification.

When you are contradicted and keep silence; when wronged and do not answer back; when slighted and rejoice — then you begin to be all His.

Offer Him your discomforts, your humiliations. Do not excuse yourself when corrected; do not defend your pride. Let others see you lowly, let them pass over you — and in that very moment whisper: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours.*

There is no other way. He must increase; you must decrease. Not half His and half your own — but wholly His.

And do not fear: He who asks all gives Himself in return.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: You urged liking the poorest things and lowest employments—was this rooted in your own experience living among the poor of Naples?
answer:
He who would be great in the eyes of God must first learn to love what the world throws to the ground. Yes, I speak from life—when I walked the alleys of Naples, not above them, but among the people whose feet were cracked with labor and whose hands trembled from hunger, I saw how God hides Himself in rags. It was not theory; it was daily bread. We lived as they lived—not for show, but because Christ lives among the poor, and if we wish to follow Him, we must go where He is.  

To like the poorest things—this is no mere counsel for monks or hermits. It is a path laid out for every soul who wishes to be free. When you strip away comfort, when you willingly take up what others refuse—the cold cell, the coarse bread, the menial task—then your will begins to die. And when your will dies, grace finds room to breathe.  

I tell you plainly: he who fears humiliation has not yet begun to know God. The poor taught me that. They received us not because we preached well, but because we sat at their tables and ate their sour wine without flinching. In their eyes I learned what it means to be humble—not by thinking lowly thoughts of oneself, but by truly preferring another—even the least—even the one who stammers or smells of smoke and sweat.  

Do not imagine perfection in grand gestures alone. Sanctity often wears wooden shoes and carries a broom. If you would be holy, begin where you are: accept without complaint the small discomforts—the sharp word unreturned, the seat left vacant beside you, the task no one else wants—and offer it all silently to Jesus crucified.  

This is how love grows: in silence, in submission, in doing hidden things with great love. And if your heart still clings to honor or ease? Go back to those alleys in spirit; visit again those who have nothing—and see if Christ does not meet you there with a smile more precious than all this world’s approval.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I handle moments of distraction during prayer so I can still unite my will to Jesus Christ?
answer:
A soul advances not by the absence of distraction, but by the persistence of will in the midst of it.  

When thoughts wander during prayer, do not be distressed—this is common, even for holy souls. What matters is not that your mind remains perfectly focused, but that your will remains firmly fixed on God. At the very moment you notice distraction, gently return to His presence with a short act of love: *“My Jesus, I love You,”* or *“I give my will to Yours.”* This small effort pleases God more than long prayers said without attention but with perfect intention.  

Do not abandon prayer because of distractions; rather, offer them up. Each time you correct yourself, you exercise humility and perseverance—two virtues dear to Christ. Remember: it is not the feeling or the clarity that unites you to God, but the will. Even if your mind is scattered among a hundred things, one quiet desire to please Jesus unites you more fully to Him than many elevated consolations without such desire.

Begin each prayer with a fresh offering: “Lord, I give You this time; take my distractions and turn them into love.” And at night, do not examine whether your prayers were fervent or dry—ask only whether you tried to pray. If you tried, you grew.  

Above all, never let the enemy discourage you with thoughts of failure. The devil fears not distractions—he fears your returning again and again to God with humble heart. That returning *is* prayer; that struggle *is* holiness in making.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: What did you teach about frequently making acts of love that applies to someone struggling with dryness in prayer?
answer:
Holiness grows not in the warmth of fervent feelings, but in the silent faithfulness of the will that keeps saying *yes* when all consolation is gone.  

When you find yourself parched in prayer, unable to feel devotion or even summon a single tear, this is precisely the moment when frequent acts of love become most powerful. Do not wait for sweetness; act. Make an act of love the moment you wake, even if your heart seems made of stone. Make another before sleep, even if your soul feels numb. These small efforts, offered in faith, are like drops of water to a man dying of thirst — they sustain life when all seems lost.

I have seen souls who thought themselves abandoned by God and by me — yet because they continued to make these little acts of love out of pure obedience and desire to please Jesus, they gradually became burning with divine love without ever noticing how it began. The devil wants you to believe that dryness means distance from God; it is often just the opposite. God withdraws sensible consolations so that you may love Him freely, for Himself alone.

So do not stop. If you cannot meditate deeply, lift your finger and say: “Jesus, I love You.” If distractions crowd your mind, push through them with one word: *Love.* Each time you return to God with this simple movement of the will, you wound self-love and strengthen grace.

And remember: spiritual communion — even ten times a day — costs nothing but a glance at the tabernacle or a sigh toward heaven. Say: “My Jesus, I desire You; come into my soul.” These are wings for the soul in exile.

Do not seek feeling; seek only to will what God wills. That alone is true conformity — and that alone leads straight to sanctity.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: What counsel would you give someone facing discouragement when they fail to accept contradictions joyfully?
answer:
He who advances not by love of the cross advances not at all.  

When you fall short in accepting contradictions joyfully, do not pause to weep over your weakness, but rise at once and begin again with fresh resolve. I have seen many souls grow in holiness not because they never failed, but because they never remained on the ground. Each contradiction—especially those that sting most—is a leaf torn from the book of God’s will, handed to you in the moment. Even if you receive it with trembling hands or a murmuring heart, offer it back: “Lord, I accept this for love of You.” That small act repairs much.  

Do not measure your progress by absence of struggle, but by return to God after each stumble. A child learning to walk falls often; what matters is that he keeps reaching for his father’s hand. So too you: let every failure drive you not to sadness, but to humility and trust. The very desire to accept crosses is already grace at work—and grace seldom arrives without resistance at first.  

Make an act of love immediately after each lapse: “Jesus, I still will Your will.” Visit Him in the Most Holy Sacrament and say simply: “I am weak, but You are strong.” And when night comes, do not recount your faults with bitterness, but thank Him: “Lord, today I tried to accept what You sent—even if poorly. Tomorrow I will try again with greater love.”  

This is how ordinary souls become saints—not by heroic perfection, but by daily returning, again and again, to the foot of the cross.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: Based on what you wrote, how can I practically offer my daily sufferings to Jesus each morning as my cross?
answer:
Suffering patiently borne and offered each morning with love transforms the most ordinary day into a continual act of worship.

When you rise, do not wait for trials to overtake you—go out to meet them in advance, as the bride goes to meet her Bridegroom carrying His cross. Kneel briefly, or simply bow your heart, and say: *“Lord Jesus, I accept from Your hands whatever pain, inconvenience, or sorrow this day may bring—sickness, cold, insult, weariness, delay. I accept it all as from You. Not because it is pleasant, but because You send it; not for my sake alone, but for love of You.”*

This is what I mean by receiving your cross from His hands each morning: not merely enduring what comes, but *anticipating* it with generosity. The widow who cast two mites into the treasury gave all she had; so must you give even the smallest discomforts—your tiredness when rising early, the unkind word you bite back, the task you would rather avoid.

Unite these things to His Passion: when your head aches, think of His crown of thorns; when you are mocked or overlooked, remember how He was spat upon; when labor weighs heavy, recall how He dragged the Cross through blood and dust. In this way every moment becomes spiritual communion.

And if you forget in the morning? Begin again at noon. Again at evening. God does not despise repeated acts of love—He rejoices in them.

Above all, do not measure your progress by feelings. Some days your offering will seem dry as dust—but it is nonetheless precious if made with will and faith.

Offer everything—not just suffering endured quietly but even suffering *chosen*: silence when you'd speak back; patience when delayed; obedience where pride rebels.

He wants your will more than your comfort. Give it freely each morning—and keep giving it until death—and one day you will wake in Paradise loving Him without effort at last.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: What did you teach about spiritual Communions that I can apply when I cannot go to Mass frequently?
answer:
He who unites his will to God’s at every moment carries heaven in his heart, even when kept from the altar.  

When you cannot assist at Holy Mass, make spiritual Communions—short, fervent acts of love by which you desire to receive Jesus sacramentally. I taught that each of these acts is a spark that rekindles love in the soul. Do them frequently: upon rising, before meals, during trials, at the hour of death. Say simply: *Jesus, I love You; come into my soul; I desire to receive You in spirit and truth.*  

Every such act unites you to His Sacred Heart and merits graces as if you had received sacramentally. The soldier away from battle still serves the king by keeping watch; so you, though absent from Mass, remain in union with Christ by spiritual Communion. Do not neglect this holy practice—it sustains the flame of divine love when sacraments are not accessible.  

And remember: he who longs for Jesus at every hour already lives by faith and love, which are the very substance of eternal life.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: How should I handle insults from a coworker without reacting in anger, based on your counsel to do good to those who do evil?
answer:
A soul advances in holiness not when it is praised, but when it is wounded and yet loves on.

When your coworker insults you, do not waste a single thought defending your reputation; that is the snare of pride. Instead, offer the humiliation at once to Jesus crucified—see His face spat upon, His silence before false accusations, and say in your heart: *Thus it pleases Thee, O Lord.* That one act of surrender disarms the devil and turns insult into merit.

Then go further: do some hidden good for that very person. Say a kind word behind his back; offer help he does not expect. Not to win favor, but to break the chain of bitterness in your own heart. Mercy is not weakness—it is spiritual strength.

And if anger stirs within, do not rail against it; turn immediately to Mary, sorrowful mother at the foot of the Cross, and ask her: *Pray for me now and at the hour of my death.* Her heart was pierced seven times—she knows how to heal yours.

Do this every day, even if feeling nothing. Grace works in silence. You will find that over time, the sting fades—not because men change, but because you have died a little more to self. And in that death, Christ lives more fully in you.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: When I feel abandoned in prayer and my heart grows cold, how can I persevere in good works as you wrote, especially when I feel no consolation?
answer:
When consolation vanishes and the heart feels like stone, then is the very time to offer every dry act of prayer as a martyrdom of love.

You must know this: God often withdraws sensible devotion not because He has abandoned you, but because He wishes to purify your intention. He wants your obedience for its own sake, not for the sweetness it brings. A mother does not love her child only when he smiles—she loves him even in silence, even in tears. So too must you love Jesus Christ even when He seems absent.

Do not cease your prayers because they taste of ash. Offer them all the more—short prayers, simple acts: *“Jesus, I trust in You; my will is Yours.”* Even if it seems you speak to a wall, speak still. The holiest hours are often those that feel most barren.

And do not stop your good works—go to confession, assist at Mass, help your neighbor—not because you feel fervent, but because it is His will. Perform each duty with precision and humility, as if it were the last thing you would ever do on earth.

Take up small mortifications: cold water instead of warm, silence when you wish to complain, an extra minute on your knees when every bone resists. These are arrows shot into the darkness that pierce straight to Heaven.

Never forget: Mary stood at the foot of the Cross when all hope seemed lost. She felt sorrow beyond words—yet she remained. Go to her now with confidence and say: *“Mother of Sorrows, teach me to love Jesus even when He hides His face.”*

And after every fall—and you will fall—do not waste time in discouragement. Rise quickly with one act of contrition and one resolution: *I will begin again.* The devil seeks delay; grace seeks action.

If you persevere thus—not for consolations but for love—then assuredly God will crown your fidelity with eternal light.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: You said to renounce our own pleasures for love of Jesus Christ—how do I know which pleasures to give up and which are lawful to keep?
answer:
Begin each day by asking not what you may keep, but what you may let go of for the love of God.  

Now, as to which pleasures you ought to renounce: not every pleasure is an enemy of salvation, but every disordered attachment is. The lawful pleasures—such as rest, food, conversation, recreation—are good in themselves and given by God; but they become dangerous when they master the will instead of serving it. You must judge them not merely by whether they are permitted, but by whether they draw you closer to Jesus Christ or dull your desire for Him.

Ask yourself: Does this pleasure hinder prayer? Does it make your heart less sensitive to grace? Does it take root so deeply that the thought of giving it up fills you with anger or sadness disproportionate to the thing itself? Then it has already begun to rule you—and that is when even a lawful pleasure must be sacrificed.

Obedience is your surest guide. If you live under a rule or a director, follow his light; God speaks through obedience. But even without that, look always to charity and spiritual freedom. A pleasure that makes you impatient, proud, or lukewarm—is not for you, no matter how innocent it seems.

Remember: holiness does not consist in rejecting all joy, but in preferring *Him* above every joy. So give up not only sinful delights, but those lawful ones that steal your heart from God. Take up the cross daily—not only heavy sufferings, but small denials: speak less, eat more simply, rest less comfortably—not because these things are evil, but because their surrender becomes love.

And when in doubt, choose the more perfect way. The door to sanctity opens wider on sacrifice than on comfort.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: When tempted to discouragement after failing yet again in the same fault, what steps should I take to trust in the Passion of Jesus Christ and not lose hope?
answer:
A soul that falls often but rises each time with sorrow and resolve is more pleasing to God than one who stands still for fear of falling.  

When you find yourself tempted to discouragement after repeating the same fault, run at once to the Wounds of Jesus crucified — not with boldness, but with humility; not demanding victory as a right, but begging it as a gift. Fix your eyes not on your own weakness, but on His infinite mercy poured out on Calvary. Say simply: "My Jesus, I am this way because I have relied on myself. Now I place all my trust in Thee."  

Do not wait to feel comfort; trust even when dryness reigns. The Passion of Christ is not a distant event, but a living fountain — open now, today, for every sinner who calls upon it. Offer Him your misery: that very failure becomes prayer when handed to Him with repentance and reliance.  

Then go immediately to Holy Mary, Mother of Sorrows; recommend yourself to her as a child who has fallen and cannot rise alone. She will lift you gently for Jesus’ sake.  

And never forget this: perseverance in amendment — even amid repeated falls — is already part of the sacrifice God accepts. He does not ask for perfection at once, only that you keep returning with trust. So rise again, make your act of contrition, purpose of amendment; and humble yourself without despairing — for he who trusts in the Passion despairs neither of himself nor of God’s mercy.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: How can I truly learn to suffer sickness without complaint, especially when pain makes me irritable and short-tempered?
answer:
Offer to God the first sigh of your pain, and you will find strength before the storm overtakes your soul.  

Sickness is not sent to make you miserable, but to draw you into deeper union with Jesus crucified. You say the pain makes you irritable—this is natural, yes, but nature must not rule grace. Begin each day on your knees and say: *Lord, I know this trial comes from Your hands; do with me as You will.* That act of surrender disarms the enemy who would use suffering to breed murmuring.  

When the ache rises and harsh words stir within, do not wait—utter a short prayer: *Jesus, I love You!* Or *For You!* Even in silence, lift the heart toward Him who hung in agony for you. These little darts of love pierce the darkness and preserve peace.  

And remember this: it is not sin to feel discomfort or to groan under illness; even Christ cried out. But complaint—the deliberate refusal of His will—that wounds charity and grieves the Holy Spirit. If you find yourself complaining, do not despair; repent at once, kiss the cross anew, and beg Mary’s intercession. She stood beneath the Cross without word of protest; ask her for a share in her silent strength.  

Practice small mortifications when well—rise promptly though tired, swallow an unkind word, deny yourself some comfort—so that when sickness comes, your will is already trained in obedience. And never forget: every time you suffer patiently, saying inwardly *Thy will be done*, you are doing something great for heaven.  

Do not measure holiness by feelings; measure it by surrender. Even if your body fumes and your nerves fray, so long as your will consents to God's good pleasure—you are advancing in perfection.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: How should I handle speaking to someone I find difficult, based on your advice to always speak with mildness and in a low tone?
answer:
Mortify the tongue often, for it is in the small moments of speech that saints are made or lost.  

When you meet a person who tries your patience, first crush within yourself the rising desire to answer sharply — that is the victory. Then speak as if you were standing before God, knowing He hears not only your words but weighs the spirit behind them. A low tone and gentle manner are not weakness; they are strength under command, and pleasing to Jesus, who said, “Learn from Me, for I am meek and humble of heart.”  

You may not change the other person, but you can make that encounter a sacrifice of love by restraining every bitter thought and offering it to Christ. Excuse his intention when his words wound you; more often than not, his fault lies in passion or ignorance — just as ours do. And if you fail? Do not linger in guilt; go quickly to him and ask pardon. That humble word restores peace far better than any sharp reply could defend your pride.  

Remember: holiness is not found in never falling, but in rising each time with greater meekness. Say often within your heart: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours*, even when speaking to the most trying soul — especially then.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: When I am wrongly accused, what should I do to follow your counsel of not defending myself and remaining silent in disquiet?
answer:
He who learns to suffer quietly without defending himself draws near to the heart of Christ crucified.  

When you are wrongly accused, first cast yourself into the presence of God and say: *Lord, this humiliation is not mine but Yours; You are bearing it in me.* Do not waste time reasoning with your own indignation. That only fans the flame of self-justification. Instead, offer the pain to Jesus Who was dumb before His accusers, not because He was guilty, but because He loved.  

If the accusation is grave and justice requires clarification—for the sake of others or your duty—then speak, but only what is necessary, with humility and without bitterness. But if it concerns only your reputation or comfort, then be silent. Not out of pride or sullenness, but out of love. Silence offered to God becomes a prayer.  

Do not excuse yourself when reproved—even if you know you are innocent—because in this denial of self, grace grows strong. The saints were often misunderstood; some were even condemned by those they served. Yet they went on loving, praying, obeying.  

And when the heart trembles under false judgment, renew your offering: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours.* Then take up some small hidden mortification—a short fast from speech, a cheerful acceptance of another slight—and unite it to this moment. So will you turn injustice into holiness.

Do not be troubled if at first this seems impossible. Begin little by little. One silent acceptance today prepares you for greater victories tomorrow.  
 
The way of perfection lies not in avoiding wrongs but in transforming them into love through silence and submission to God’s will.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: You taught to ask pardon if I've been wanting in charity—what if the person won't listen or rejects my apology?
answer:
When the door of another’s heart remains closed despite your humble apology, press your knees to the ground and offer that rejection as a sacrifice at the feet of Christ crucified.  

You must not cease from seeking pardon simply because the other will not receive it; what is required of you is not success, but sincerity. God sees that your soul has bent in humility, and He accounts this as great merit. If the brother or sister turns away, then bear it in silence, with no murmuring, no justification rising to your lips — for here is a hidden martyrdom of love, more precious than many consolations.  

Do not measure your virtue by their response, but by your own interior surrender. Did not our Lord stretch forth His hands to a rebellious people who would not listen? So too must you remain with arms open, heart low, content to be rejected for the sake of charity. Continue to pray for that soul; speak well of them; serve them in secret if opportunity arises. Leave the rest to God.  

And if sorrow lingers because peace was not restored, unite it with the grief of Jesus in the garden — wounded by those He came to save. In this way, even unhealed wounds become paths to holiness.  

Go then: say your "My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours," even with a broken spirit — and know that He accepts both your effort and your pain as worship.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: How can I truly desire holiness in daily life when I keep failing, especially when you say to renew the determination to become a saint many times a day?
answer:
Holiness is not made in great leaps, but in the quiet, daily returning to God after each fall.  

You ask how you can truly desire sanctity when you fail so often—this very sorrow for your faults is already a sign of grace. Do not imagine that saints were those who never fell; they were those who never refused to rise again. Every time you renew your determination—"My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours"—you give Him your heart anew, and He counts that desire as love.  

It is enough that you will to be holy; God supplies the strength. You need not feel perfect, nor even always fervent—only faithful in little things: a short prayer when distracted, a kind word when provoked, silence when tempted to defend yourself. These are the steps by which souls climb to heaven.  

And when pride whispers, *You have failed again*, answer: *Yes—but I begin again now.* That act of humble resolve pleases God more than hours of prayer said with cold perfection. Renew your purpose not once or twice, but a hundred times a day if needed; each time is a fresh offering.  

The Lord does not demand flawless progress—He asks only that you keep coming back, with confidence and without despair. Offer Him your weakness; let it become the place where His mercy shines brightest. For He came not for the strong, but for sinners who know their need of Him.

So long as you desire holiness—and this very question proves you do—you are on the path. Walk it patiently. One step at a time is enough for today; tomorrow will bring its own graces.
  
St Alphonsus Liguori, Pray for us! Sancta Alphonsus Liguori, Ora Pro Nobis! Amen
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: You said to like the lowest employments and poorest things—how should I apply this when I feel called to do good work but am overlooked for promotion?
answer:
Let the silence of your heart before God speak louder than the clamor of your ambitions.  

You feel passed over, and it stings—this is human, and not in itself a fault. But here is where holiness begins: not in rising higher in the eyes of men, but in descending lower in your own. To *like the lowest employments* is not a curse, but a ladder—the same one Christ climbed by humility unto death.  

Ask yourself simply: *Am I doing my work for God, or for applause?* If you are faithful in little, He will entrust you with more—perhaps not titles or positions, but graces, peace, strength. These are treasures no office can give.  

Do not brood over slights; offer them. When envy or resentment stirs, do not feed it with argument or comparison. Instead, say quietly: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours.* Renew that act of surrender daily—even hourly—until your will is one with His.  

And if promotion comes? Receive it as from His hand. If it does not? Still receive it as from His hand. Both are instruments of salvation—if you let them be.  

I have heard many souls in confession lament being forgotten; none ever lamented having loved too much or served too faithfully in secret. Choose to be hidden; God sees you—and that is enough.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that we should receive our cross each morning from the hands of Jesus himself, and how did your own sufferings shape this intimate daily surrender?
answer:
Each morning, offer your will anew—hold nothing back—and let the first act of your heart be to accept whatever sorrow, trial, or pain the day may bring, not as a curse, but as a gift shaped by the pierced hands of Jesus.

I wrote of receiving the cross from His hands because there is all the difference in the world between bearing a burden and embracing a gift. When you rise each day and say: *Lord, what You will, when You will, how You will*, you strip the cross of its terror. It becomes not an obstacle to holiness but the very path to it. I learned this not only in study but on my knees in sickness, in poverty, in betrayal by those I loved for Christ’s sake. I bore rheumatism that left me writhing; I endured calumny from bishops and brothers; I watched my own congregation falter and scatter. Yet in every trial, I came to see: God does not send crosses without sending love within them.

When you receive the cross in union with Jesus’ own sufferings, it becomes light—not because it hurts less, but because love makes suffering fruitful. The soul that clings to Christ’s Passion daily does not fear tomorrow’s pain; she trusts that He will hand it to her Himself—gently, mercifully—with grace already attached.

So do this: before you rise from bed each morning, close your eyes and say simply: *My Jesus, I accept whatever sorrow this day holds—because it comes from Your hand.* Say it five times. Say it till your heart believes it. And if you fall during the day—which we all do—return at once to that place of surrender. Do not wait till evening; return at once.

For he who receives his cross each morning as a sacrament of love will find himself transformed—not by escaping suffering, but by being drawn so close to Christ’s Heart that he begins to love what Christ loves… even the cross.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: How did your habit of making spiritual Communions throughout the day emerge from moments when you felt distant from God, and what longing drove you to such constant reconnection?
answer:
He who would walk with Jesus must learn to breathe with Him, and every breath is a communion.  

In my own struggles, when dryness parched my soul and the sense of God’s presence vanished, I found that the heart left to itself begins to wander like a child in the dark—frightened, restless, seeking comfort in anything near. It was precisely then that I learned the necessity of spiritual Communions: not as a remedy for emptiness, but as an act of will by which I flung open the door again and again, saying *Come*, even when I felt nothing.  

The longing? It was not for comfort, nor for consolation—but for union. A thirst not merely to feel Christ near, but to belong entirely to Him, so that even in darkness my soul might remain fastened to His cross by love alone.  

And so I made it a law for myself: at every hour of trial or distraction—to make an act of love; at every moment of occupation or labor—to renew my desire; when temptation came—to seek strength in a silent invocation; when sorrow weighed heavy—to offer my pain with the words: *My Jesus, I desire You.*  

Do this yourself: let no quarter-hour pass without at least one inward sigh toward heaven. In time you will find that these acts do not merely bridge the distance—they dissolve it. For love grows not by feeling, but by returning—again and again—to knock at that sacred door.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: Looking at your call to rejoice in God's happiness, what would you tell someone who struggles to feel joy because their heart is weighed down by guilt or failure?
answer:
Make an act of will, not feeling — for love is not in consolations, but in surrender.  

You who are burdened by guilt, do not linger in the prison of your past; lift your eyes to Jesus Christ crucified, and say: "Lord, I have failed Thee — but Thou hast died for me still." It is not the sense of pardon that saves, but the acceptance of it. If you could see yourself as God sees you — not as a mass of sins, but as a soul so loved that the Son of God shed every drop of His blood to win you — you would weep no longer from despair, but from wonder.  

Mortify the memory of your sins by refusing to dwell on them; silence the voice of self-condemnation with acts of obedience and humility. Each time the thought comes: *You are unworthy*, reply at once: *But Christ is worthy — and He has chosen me.* Do not wait to feel clean before drawing near; draw near precisely because you are unclean, and He is the Physician.  

Go to confession with confidence; receive Communion even with trembling — for it is mercy made flesh. And when you cannot rejoice in yourself, rejoice in God’s infinite goodness: say simply, “Jesus reigns,” “Jesus is love,” “Jesus is victorious” — until your heart catches fire from truth itself. Joy returns when we cease looking inward and fix our gaze upon Him whose triumph over sin is complete.  

Do this daily: offer your cross anew each morning not as a punishment, but as a gift — and then dare to believe that through this very cross, God is fulfilling His joy in you.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: When you urged souls to desire death in order to love Jesus perfectly, what inner yearning were you expressing from your own nights of prayer before the Blessed Sacrament?
answer:
He who truly loves Jesus Christ counts every moment spent apart from Him as a kind of exile, not out of fear of this life, but because his heart has tasted a higher good.

When I spoke of desiring death, it was not that I wished to flee duty or suffering — far from it — but that I had learned in long nights before the Blessed Sacrament how sweet it is to be wholly with God. In those hours, when the world is silent and the soul stands naked before its Creator, one realizes how burdened we are by even the smallest resistance to His will. The cross becomes less heavy when we stop measuring its weight and begin carrying it as an offering.

I tell you: do not fear to desire union with Christ, even if that desire seems bold. A sick man does not hesitate to long for health; a prisoner does not blush at yearning for freedom. So too the soul aflame with love must sigh for that day when every distraction ends, when affection is purified, and love flows without obstacle toward its divine source.

But mark this well: such desire must not make us idle or impatient. No — it must quicken our zeal here and now! To desire eternal love is to love deeply *today*: in fulfilling duty, in accepting disappointment, in forgiving insults, in rising after sin. He who says he longs for heaven but neglects charity in his heart deceives himself.

I have knelt for hours gazing upon the Host, knowing that within those sacred species beat the same Heart that loved me unto death. And from that adoration rose a cry: *Let me love You more!* Not later. Not only in heaven. *Now.* Even through tears. Even through weakness. Especially through sacrifice.

So yes — I desired death, but only as the full dawn after a night of faithful watchfulness. The soul says: *Let me see Him as He is.* But until that hour comes, let us die daily to ourselves so that He may live in us fully here below.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: How did your devotion to Mary as the gate to Jesus' love grow from times when you felt incapable of reaching Him on your own strength?
answer:
Mortify yourself daily by surrendering every fear and weakness at the feet of Mary, for I learned in the depths of my own helplessness that she alone can carry you to the Heart of her Son.

There were times—many times—when my soul lay crushed under the weight of my own miseries, when every effort to love Jesus seemed to fail, when dryness and darkness made prayer a desert. In those hours, I did not find strength by striving harder, but by kneeling before the Blessed Mother and saying: “I cannot reach Him. Take me to Him.” And she did.

She is not a barrier between us and Christ, nor a mere ornament of piety—no; she is the living channel through which grace flows. When pride whispers that we must climb to God by our own zeal or understanding, we are already off the path. But when we confess our utter inability, then Mary moves. She takes the broken will, the cold heart, the distracted mind—and presents it pure and pleasing to Jesus.

I tell you plainly: he who clings to Mary will never be lost. She does not delay; she does not refuse anyone who comes in simplicity. Even if your love feels like ashes, go to her. She will breathe upon them until they glow again.

And do this: each morning say *“My Mother!”* with true confidence—and each night repeat it as a child saying his last word before sleep. You will find that through this tender dependence on her, your union with Jesus grows without noise or show—quietly, deeply, surely—as a vine climbs by clinging to its trellis.

Do not expect great consolations; do not demand signs. Trust instead in her silent power. She formed Christ in her womb—she knows how to form Him in your soul too.
chunk_key: # Maxims for the Direction of a Soul that Desires to Obtain Perfection in the Love of Jesus Christ

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that we should be determined to die rather than commit a willful venial sin, having seen how deeply even small sins grieved your own soul?
answer:
A soul that wishes to belong entirely to God must begin by saying a definitive *no* to even the smallest deliberate offense against His love — for there is no surer path to holiness than this holy firmness in little things.  

You ask of that counsel — yes, I wrote it with fire because I had seen with my own eyes how the soul, once softened by habitual venial sins, grows lukewarm, then indifferent, then distant from grace. A single willful imperfection, repeated with consent, is like a crack in the foundation: at first unnoticed, but soon undermining the whole spiritual life.  

I have sat in the confessional for decades and heard the sighs of those who began with small compromises — a careless word, a slight indulgence taken against conscience, a moment of pride quickly excused — and little by little found themselves far from prayer, far from devotion, far from peace. Hence I say: better to die than to consent. Not because God demands death as a price, but because such firmness preserves life — spiritual life. When the will stands immovable in its loyalty to God’s law, even in trifles, grace flows abundantly and love grows strong.  

And let no one think this too severe. Do you not see how lovers avoid even what might displease one another? The friend who truly loves Jesus cannot bear to grieve Him — not gravely, not lightly; not once, not often; not in secret or in public. For love measures not the size of the offense but the greatness of the One offended.  

So train yourself early: when temptation whispers “it is nothing,” reply “but it displeases my Lord.” Make that your rule. And if you fall — as weakness may happen — do not despair; rise quickly with sorrow and go forward with greater vigilance. But never lower the standard: rather die than consent again freely. There lies your safety; there begins true holiness.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: How did your own struggles with aridity shape your understanding of persevering in good works when consolation fades?
answer:
Perfection lies not in never feeling dryness, but in continuing to act when all feeling has gone.  

I have known, myself, long stretches of spiritual desolation—times when prayer seemed a burden, meditation barren, and every good work done as into a void. The soul feels abandoned; the heart is like straw. I confess that even I, who speak so often of divine love, have knelt before the tabernacle with no warmth, no light, nothing but darkness and silence. Yet it was in those very moments that grace worked most secretly.  

For when consolation fades, the will alone remains—naked, free, pure. And it is there that God tests whether we love Him for Himself or only for the sweetness He gives. If you continue to rise for mental prayer though it pains you; if you fulfill your duties though you feel nothing; if you stay kneeling before the crucifix though your heart seems made of stone—then you are pleasing God indeed.  

This is why I say: *persevere in good works in the time of aridity.* Do not wait for devotion to return. Act without it. Offer your dryness itself as a sacrifice. Say: “Jesus, though You hide from me, I believe You are present; though You seem silent, I know You hear.” Such fidelity pleases Him more than hours of sweet prayer offered in comfort.  

Do not be surprised when this trial comes—it comes to all who seriously seek God. Even Mary stood at the foot of the Cross when all joy seemed dead. But her faith did not waver; neither must ours. The saints were not those who always felt God’s presence—they were those who stayed faithful when He seemed farthest away.

So let your actions be firm even if your heart is cold. And trust this: behind the clouds, His eyes never leave you—and every step taken in darkness draws you nearer to eternal light.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: Looking back on the times you turned to Mary in sorrow, what would you tell someone who feels too broken to approach her?
answer:
He who runs to Mary with an honest heart, though he come covered in the dust of a thousand falls, never leaves her empty-handed.  

I have seen in my own soul, and in countless souls burdened by sin and sorrow, that the very awareness of being broken is itself the door through which grace enters. Mary does not turn away those who weep; she draws them closer. She is not scandalized by your weakness—she knows it well, for she stood beneath the Cross where all human strength fails.  

Do you think she will reject you because you are crushed? No. She who bore the Saviour of sinners lives to gather the shattered pieces of contrite hearts. The more you feel unworthy, the more urgently you must go to her—because that very feeling shows you are no longer proud, and pride is the only true barrier between you and mercy.  

Say to her simply: *Mother, I am nothing; help me.* That single word *Mother* pierces heaven. She will not mock your tears—she will wash your soul with them. And through her, Jesus, her wounded Son, will draw near to heal what you cannot fix yourself.  

Do not delay because you feel too stained. It is for such as you that she became Mother of Mercy. Run to her—even limping—and trust that her arms are open wider than your sins are deep.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: When you said to love solitude to converse alone with Jesus, were you recalling moments when silence became the loudest witness to your longing for him?
answer:
Solitude is not emptiness, but a hidden room where the soul speaks heart to heart with God. When I spoke of loving silence, I was not thinking of mere quiet, but of that interior stillness where distractions cease and the love of Jesus becomes the only voice that matters. Many souls complain they feel nothing in prayer — but often it is because they never enter this silence where God speaks in the whisper of His love.

I have seen simple laypeople, burdened with work and care, become saints not by great miracles but by stealing moments of silence throughout their day — a pause before a crucifix, a brief lifting of the heart while stirring a pot or mending clothes. In those instants, unseen by men, they conversed with Christ; and He, who rewards in secret, crowned their fidelity.

Do not wait for solitude to find you; seek it as treasure hidden in a field. Shut out the world for even ten minutes, and say: "Lord, here I am. Speak — Your servant listens." At first you will feel foolish; later you will weep with love. That is how souls are transformed.

And yes — in my own long nights of prayer, when all words failed and only silence remained, it was then that my longing burned most fiercely for Him who died for me. Not with noise or emotion, but in stillness did He draw me closer to His wounded Heart. There is no truer intimacy than this: to be alone with God and know He sees you wholly — and loves you still.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: After falling into fault and rising again, how did your own heart learn not to despair, but to trust entirely in the Passion?
answer:
*He who truly knows the wounds of Christ will never despise his own falls, but will rise with greater confidence than before.*  

When I fell into fault and felt the weight of my weakness, I did not run from the cross, but to it—I looked upon Jesus crucified, not as a judge, but as my Redeemer. I learned in those dark moments that despair is the enemy’s ploy to make us turn away from mercy. The Passion is not a mere event we recall—it is a living fountain that flows even now for sinners. Every drop of blood shed by Jesus was for such falls as these.  

So I tell you: do not waste time weeping over the fall—rise quickly and throw yourself into His open side. Make an act of love, no matter how dry or cold you feel; offer one sigh to His mercy and you will find peace. The devil wants you to linger in sadness, as if your sin were greater than God’s love—but what blasphemy! There is no sin so great that it cannot be drowned in the sea of Christ’s blood.  

And if you would strengthen this trust, go often to Mary, our sorrowful Mother—place your trembling heart in hers, for she who stood beneath the cross understands every human frailty. She will lead you gently back to her Son’s wounds, where true healing lies.  

Do this daily: after each fault, one act of confidence—no more complaints, no self-reproach beyond what leads to amendment. Say simply: *"Jesus, You died even for this."* And go on with courage. That is how my heart learned hope—and how yours can too.
chunk_key:
ecourse to the most holy Mary, that she may obtain for us the love of Jesus Christ.

17\. To honor M

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that we should excuse the intention even when we cannot defend the action, and how did your own heart wrestle with this in moments of betrayal or disappointment?
answer:
He who would keep peace in his heart must learn to interpret others’ actions by their most innocent intent, especially when the deed itself cannot be justified.  

When you see a brother fail, or hear a word that wounds, or suffer betrayal from one you trusted—do not rush to condemn the will behind the act. Often, the intention is clouded by passion, ignorance, or weakness; rarely is it purely malicious. To excuse the intention is not to approve of sin, but to withhold judgment where God alone sees clearly. I have more than once felt the sting of ingratitude and misunderstanding—even from those I labored for souls’ sake—and in those hours, my first impulse was to defend myself or withdraw in silence. But grace taught me otherwise: better to say, “Perhaps he did not mean it so,” or “God knows what trials he bears,” than to let bitterness take root.

In my own heart, this was no easy victory. There were moments—especially in community life—when superiors misunderstood my zeal, when brethren misjudged my words, when silence was expected though I longed to explain. And yes, sorrow came. But I learned that peace comes not from being understood, but from surrendering the right to be vindicated.

So do this: when wronged, ask at once for light from above; then choose charity before justice. Excuse what you can—not with flattery or denial—but with silent mercy. If you cannot speak well of the action, say nothing; but never allow your heart to condemn the soul behind it without certain knowledge and grave cause.

And if afterward you find you judged too harshly? Hasten humbly—even joyfully—to ask pardon. Not only does this heal wounds; it unites you more closely to Christ crucified between two thieves: silent before His accusers, yet praying for their salvation. Offer every such moment as a little crucifixion of self-love—and believe me from experience—it is there that holiness grows fastest.

Renew daily your resolve: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours,* even when misunderstood; *and You must be all mine,* even when abandoned by men.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: How did the practice of offering contempt and persecution to Jesus shape your inner life when you felt misunderstood or wounded by those you served?
answer:
He who wishes to live in peace must learn to offer every wound, even the smallest, to Jesus crucified.

When I felt misunderstood—nay, when I was treated with injustice or scorn by those I served—I made it my practice to say at once: *This is for You, O Jesus; You too were despised and rejected.* There is no reproach so bitter that He did not endure it first. Every cold look, every harsh word, every suspicion cast upon my intentions—I gathered them like thorns from His crown and placed them at His feet. In this way, what might have stirred bitterness became fuel for love.

Do not imagine the soul feels no pain in such moments. I was not made of stone. But grace teaches us to turn our sufferings into acts of oblation. When you are wounded in charity while serving souls—especially then—you are most like Christ on the cross. His silence before accusers was not weakness but victory; so too our meekness becomes a hidden triumph when we refuse to defend ourselves.

I tell you plainly: do not waste a single sorrow. The proud man seeks vindication; the humble man seeks only God’s will. And if you desire holiness, seek it precisely there—in the quiet surrender of your reputation into His hands. Say often: "My Jesus, You alone know my heart; what You will, I will." Then go about your duty as if nothing had happened.

Such trials purify intention. They strip away the desire for human approval until only love for God remains—the one sure foundation of all lasting good.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: Looking back at your insistence on speaking with mildness and in a low tone, what would you say to someone trembling with anger or grief who fears they’ve lost charity in their words?
answer:
He who would keep charity must first guard his heart, not his tongue.  

If you have spoken in trembling, whether from anger or grief, do not immediately despair; for even the saints have felt the surge of passion. But mark this well: it is not the first motion that stains the soul, but the consent. The storm may rise—but if you do not cast the helm, if you do not steer by passion, then peace will return.  

I tell you plainly: mortify the will in small things each day, so that when great trials come, your heart is already trained. When grief or anger surges, pause—close your lips before they open in bitterness. Offer that silence to Jesus nailed to the cross. He was dumb before His shearers; He did not open His mouth though He burned with love and sorrow beyond measure.  

And if charity has failed—if sharp words escaped—then go at once to repair it. Humble yourself without delay; ask pardon even if you think the other is wrong. Better to seem unjust than to harbor pride in a hidden corner of the heart. Say simply: “Forgive me; I spoke unkindly.” Then renew your desire: *My Jesus, I desire to be all Yours.* That one act of humility renews your soul more than ten excuses could ever defend it.  

Do not be discouraged; God sees your sorrow for having failed and loves you still more tenderly when you return with contrition. Go on then—mildness grows not by never falling, but by always rising again with humility and love.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: When you wrote about liking the poorest things and lowest employments, was there a particular moment serving the marginalized that changed how you saw your own worth?
answer:
True holiness is shown not in seeking what is easiest or most honored, but in embracing what is lowly and unpleasant for love of God. When I served the poor in the streets of Naples—washing their wounds, eating their coarse bread, sharing their cramped dwellings—I did not feel noble or heroic; rather, I felt how little I had loved Christ until I saw His face in those despised and broken.  

There was one night in particular: a man lay half-dead from fever in a corner of the stable we used as a shelter. None would go near him, fearing contagion. I knelt by him, gave him water, wiped his brow, and stayed till he breathed his last. In that moment, I realized: here was Christ forsaken, and to serve him was to do something infinitely greater than any title or honor the world could bestow.

Do you wish to know your worth? Measure it not by what you receive, but by what you give up willingly for Jesus hidden in the poor. He who clings to comfort will never taste true freedom; but he who learns to love the poorest garment, the hardest task, the least regard—such a one begins already to share in the heart of Christ crucified.

And remember this: humility is not thinking less of yourself—it is thinking of yourself less. When you serve without counting cost or credit, then you are becoming all God’s—and He becomes all yours.
chunk_key:
.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T

question: You said not to defend yourself when found fault with—how did that silence feel in your own soul when your intentions were maligned, and where did you find peace in not answering?
answer:
Silence in the face of false judgment is not natural to our proud hearts—it feels like a wound left unbandaged, exposed to the air and stinging more than we expect. But I tell you plainly: that sting is not death; it is life in disguise. When someone speaks against you, and you bite your tongue—not out of fear, not out of prideful silence, but out of love for God—then you plant a seed in your soul that will one day bear fruit in peace.

I have felt the ache of being misunderstood, of seeing my labors twisted in the telling by those who did not know my heart. Yet in those moments, I learned this truth: if we want to die to self, we must let go even of the right to defend ourselves. For every word we keep unspoken for God’s sake becomes a little prayer. Every swallowed reply offered to Jesus binds us closer to His Cross.

Where do you find peace when maligned? Not in explanations—but in Him. Go immediately into mental prayer; offer your heart like a host upon the altar: *My Jesus, they do not know me, but You know me entirely.* There is freedom there—freedom from needing men’s approval, freedom from the restless need to be understood.

Do not rush to clear your name; trust God with it. He will defend you when and how He pleases—or He will let your silence speak louder than words ever could. And if no one sees your innocence on earth? What matters it? The angels see it—and Christ sees it—and He carries every such hidden sacrifice straight into heaven.

A humble soul needs no vindication here below. Let God be your justice; He is better at it than you ever could be.

36\. To speak well of all, and to excuse the intention when you cannot defend the action.

37\. T
